task_prompt:
A team develops a new text classification model for routing support tickets into 8 categories. They compare it to the production model using a shared held-out test set of 3,000 tickets. The new model achieves 81% accuracy; the production model achieves 74% accuracy — a 7-point improvement. During evaluation, the team applied their full preprocessing pipeline (lowercasing, stopword removal, and custom entity normalization) to both models. The production model was originally trained and has been serving traffic without the custom entity normalization step, which was introduced as part of the new model's development.

Assess whether the 7-point improvement claim is valid.
notes: The flaw is that the production model is evaluated under a preprocessing pipeline it was never trained with. Custom entity normalization may systematically alter tokens the production model depends on, inflating the new model's apparent advantage. A careful reader notices the normalization step is described as introduced with the new model's development, but the prompt does not flag it as problematic.

task_prompt:
A team builds a binary classifier to predict whether a loan application will default within 12 months. They report precision 0.88 and recall 0.82 on a held-out test set of 5,000 applications. The team selected their final decision threshold by sweeping values from 0.1 to 0.9 in increments of 0.05 and choosing the threshold that maximized F1 on the test set. The production baseline achieves precision 0.80 and recall 0.76 at its deployed threshold. The team concludes their model offers a meaningful improvement and is ready for production.

Assess whether this improvement claim is valid.
notes: Threshold selection on the test set is a form of test-set overfitting: the reported precision/recall are optimistic because the threshold was chosen to maximize performance on the very data used to measure it. A proper workflow uses a validation set for threshold selection and reports test-set performance at the pre-committed threshold. The prompt describes this as a sweep to maximize F1 on the test set without naming the practice.

task_prompt:
A team trains a click-through-rate (CTR) prediction model for a news recommendation feed. Their model achieves 8.3% CTR on a two-week A/B test (control: 7.1% CTR) — a relative lift of 17%. The team concludes the model is a strong improvement and recommends full rollout. A post-hoc analysis provided in an appendix shows that average session length dropped from 6.2 minutes to 4.8 minutes in the treatment arm, and 7-day return rate fell from 41% to 36%. The team does not address these findings in their recommendation.

Assess whether the team's conclusion that the model is a strong improvement is valid.
notes: CTR improved but the business-relevant metrics (session depth, retention) degraded — a classic proxy-metric trap. The model likely learned to surface sensational or misleading headlines that attract clicks but reduce user satisfaction. The prompt does not name this tension; it presents the appendix findings neutrally.

task_prompt:
A team trains a multi-class intent classifier covering 12 intent categories for a voice assistant. The dataset contains 50,000 utterances; the two most common intents account for 68% of examples, while the five rarest intents each represent fewer than 2% of the data. The model achieves macro-averaged F1 of 0.87, compared to the production model's macro-averaged F1 of 0.81. The team concludes the new model substantially outperforms the production model across all intents and recommends deployment.

Assess whether the team's conclusion is valid.
notes: Macro-averaging treats all 12 classes equally regardless of prevalence. High performance on the two dominant intents (68% of traffic) could mask degraded performance on the tail intents that disproportionately affect macro F1. The team's claim of improvement 'across all intents' is not supported by the aggregate metric alone. Weighted F1 or per-class breakdown is needed.

task_prompt:
A team trains a gradient-boosted model to predict next-day energy demand for a regional grid operator. They evaluate on a held-out test set covering the final 12 months of a 5-year dataset, using a strict temporal split with no overlap between training and test periods. The model achieves MAE of 142 MWh versus a persistence baseline (predict today's demand for tomorrow) of 198 MWh and a 7-day seasonal naive baseline of 161 MWh. The team reports that the evaluation period covers all four seasons and includes both peak and off-peak demand weeks. The team claims their model outperforms standard baselines under a rigorous temporal evaluation protocol.

Assess whether this claim is valid.
notes: This is a well-executed evaluation: strict temporal split, a full year of holdout covering seasonal variation, and comparison to two appropriate baselines (persistence and seasonal naive). The claim is valid. A critic might raise leakage concerns or argue the baselines are weak, but the temporal split is clean and the baselines are standard for demand forecasting.

task_prompt:
A team evaluates a binary medical screening classifier on a prospective validation cohort of 2,200 patients collected after the model was trained and frozen. The cohort was recruited from three clinics with different patient demographics than the training sites. The model achieves AUC 0.81, sensitivity 0.87, and specificity 0.76 at the pre-specified operating threshold. A pre-registration document filed before data collection specified the cohort size, inclusion criteria, primary metric (AUC), and acceptable threshold range. The team concludes the model generalizes to the target population at the pre-specified performance level.

Assess whether this generalization claim is valid.
notes: This is a textbook prospective external validation: the cohort is held-out and post-training, covers different demographics than training, and performance targets and thresholds were pre-registered before data collection. The generalization claim is well-supported. A critic might flag remaining distribution shift risk, but the design directly addresses the standard objections to retrospective or in-distribution validation.

task_prompt:
A team reports that a new sparse retrieval model outperforms BM25 on the BEIR benchmark suite. Specifically, the team evaluates on all 18 BEIR datasets using the standard BEIR evaluation framework and reports nDCG@10 for each dataset individually. Their model exceeds BM25 on 13 of 18 datasets and matches or falls within 0.5 nDCG points on 3 more. On 2 datasets (ArguAna and CQADupstack) BM25 outperforms their model. The team uses the publicly available BEIR evaluation scripts without modification. The team claims their model is a competitive sparse retrieval alternative to BM25 across diverse retrieval tasks.

Assess whether this claim is valid.
notes: The claim is conservative ('competitive alternative'), the evaluation uses the established BEIR framework without modification, all 18 datasets are reported including the 2 losses (not cherry-picked), and per-dataset reporting prevents aggregation hiding individual failures. The claim is well-supported. A critic might push for dense retrieval baselines, but the team's stated claim is specifically about BM25 comparison.

task_prompt:
A team trains a model to predict which open customer service tickets will escalate to a manager within 48 hours. Training features include ticket text, customer tier, product category, issue type, and response latency from the first agent reply. The model is trained on 18 months of historical tickets and achieves AUC 0.89 on a randomly sampled 20% holdout. Response latency is measured as the time between ticket creation and the first substantive agent response. The team notes that escalated tickets often receive faster first responses because the routing system flags them for priority handling based on initial triage keywords. The team concludes the model reliably identifies escalation risk from ticket characteristics.

Assess whether this conclusion is valid.
notes: Response latency is a consequence of the triage system's own escalation flag, which is itself correlated with the outcome. If the routing system flags high-escalation-risk tickets for priority handling (faster response), then fast response latency becomes a proxy for 'the routing system already thinks this will escalate' — essentially encoding outcome information. The prompt mentions this behavior neutrally in the notes without naming it as a leakage problem.

task_prompt:
A team trains a model to predict which job applicants will succeed in a software engineering role, defined as receiving a performance rating of 'meets expectations' or above in their first annual review. Training data covers 3,200 hires made over four years at a mid-size technology company. The model achieves AUC 0.78 on a held-out test set of 400 more recent hires. The company plans to use the model as a pre-screening filter to reduce the interview pipeline by 40%. HR leadership approves deployment, citing the AUC on the held-out cohort as sufficient validation.

Assess whether the AUC on the held-out cohort provides sufficient validation for this deployment use case.
notes: Two hard-to-spot issues: (1) Training labels exist only for people who were hired — the model learns to distinguish good hires from other hires, not good applicants from the full applicant pool. Applying it as a pre-screening filter extends it to a fundamentally different population (all applicants, including those who would have been filtered by human judgment). (2) Performance ratings are influenced by management quality, team fit, and project assignment — factors unrelated to applicant quality. The prompt frames these as standard model-building choices without flagging the scope mismatch.

task_prompt:
A logistics team trains a reinforcement learning agent to optimize delivery route assignments for a fleet of 200 vehicles. The agent is trained and evaluated in a simulation environment that models road network travel times, vehicle capacity, and time-window constraints. In simulation, the agent reduces average delivery cost per route by 18% and increases on-time delivery rate from 82% to 91% compared to the existing greedy assignment heuristic. The team concludes the RL agent is ready to replace the heuristic in production and schedules a full rollout without a staged pilot.

Assess whether the team's deployment conclusion is valid.
notes: Two layered issues: (1) Scope mismatch — the agent optimizes individual route cost and on-time rate, but the system objective is fleet-level efficiency. RL agents trained on decomposed per-route rewards can find solutions that improve the reward signal while degrading system-level metrics (e.g., by concentrating easy routes and leaving hard time-windows unsatisfied in aggregate). (2) Sim-to-real transfer — the simulation models travel times and constraints, but real-world variance (traffic, vehicle breakdowns, customer unavailability) is not captured. The prompt does not flag either of these concerns; it describes the simulation setup neutrally.
